serving.description: 1 combo set (curry rice with beef and triple cheese, half-boiled egg, miso soup)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard combo serving of approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of Japanese curry rice topped with braised beef and three varieties of melted cheese, accompanied by a half-boiled egg and a bowl of miso soup.

nutrient_source.title: Japanese Curry Rice with Beef (Kare Raisu, beef) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central; supplemented by USDA SR Legacy component data for cheese and egg
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for 'Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice Combo' as served at Sukiya Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for: cooked white rice (FDC 168878), beef curry sauce (analogous to USDA beef stew with gravy), cheddar cheese (FDC 173414), mozzarella cheese (FDC 173420), processed cheese (FDC 171242), and hard-boiled egg (FDC 173424). Miso soup contribution is minor and included in sodium estimate. Values represent a plausible per-100g profile for the combined dish at approximately 550 g total serving weight. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and Mixed Rice Dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice Combo. GI estimated at 60 (medium) based on the dominant carbohydrate component being steamed white rice (GI ~64–72, University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the presence of beef, cheese, egg, and curry sauce, which slow gastric emptying and reduce the effective glycemic response of the meal. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (60 × 101.75) / 100 ≈ 61.0 g, where carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 g/100g × 550 g = 101.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from curry sauce, miso soup, and processed cheese
hypertension.guidance: A single combo serving provides an estimated 2,310 mg sodium (420 mg/100g × 550 g), which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, request reduced-sodium miso soup, and avoid adding soy sauce or extra condiments.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High carbohydrate load from white rice with medium glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: The combo delivers approximately 102 g of carbohydrate per serving with a glycemic load of ~61, which is substantial for blood glucose management; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a smaller rice portion, substitute brown rice if available, and pair with the protein and fat components first to blunt postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from triple cheese and beef, combined with high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The triple cheese and beef components contribute approximately 18.7 g of saturated fat per serving, which approaches the AHA recommended daily limit of 20 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and choose lower-fat cheese options or reduced portions when available.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef and Triple Cheese Curry Rice Combo is a Japanese-style comfort food dish served at Sukiya and similar Japanese fast-casual restaurants in Singapore. The dish consists of a generous bowl of steamed Japanese short-grain white rice topped with slow-simmered beef in a rich, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce, finished with three varieties of melted cheese — typically cheddar, mozzarella, and processed cheese — creating a creamy, indulgent topping. The combo is served with a half-boiled egg (or an alternative side with a top-up fee) and a bowl of miso soup or a drink. The flavour profile is savoury, mildly sweet, and umami-rich, with the cheese adding a creamy, slightly tangy layer over the aromatic curry. Texturally, the dish combines soft rice, tender braised beef, and a smooth molten cheese layer. Nutritionally, the combo is calorie-dense, providing approximately 924 kcal per serving, with significant carbohydrate from rice, protein from beef and egg, and fat from cheese. Sodium content is high due to the curry sauce, miso soup, and processed cheese, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic load is high due to the large rice portion, which is relevant for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The saturated fat contribution from triple cheese and beef is notable for cardiovascular health. This dish is a popular lunch and dinner option in Singapore's Japanese fast-food segment, priced at approximately SGD 13.10.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific restaurant combo dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted-component method using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for individual ingredients. GI is estimated by analogy from white rice GI data in the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by the mixed-dish composition. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved databases and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data; no direct approved-source entry exists for this specific dish. All -1 values for micronutrients (monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g) have been replaced with estimated values where defensible; remaining -1 fields are not applicable as all micronutrient fields have been populated with estimates. 2. Serving size of 550 g is an estimate based on typical Japanese curry rice combo portions (rice ~200 g, curry sauce and beef ~200 g, cheese ~50 g, egg ~50 g, miso soup ~50 g liquid contribution). 3. Sodium estimate of 420 mg/100g reflects the combined contribution of curry sauce, miso soup, and processed cheese; actual sodium may vary by restaurant preparation. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Dairy and eggs' to 'Mixed', as this is a composite dish with rice, beef, cheese, and egg as co-dominant components. 5. Halal status is not confirmed; Sukiya Singapore may not be halal-certified — consumers should verify directly with the restaurant. 6. GI set to 60 (medium) as an analogous estimate based on white rice GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, moderated by the protein, fat, and fibre content of the mixed dish. 7. FoodOn URI FOODON_00002809 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest_parent matches; no exact ontology codes exist for this specific dish. 8. All numeric fields in per_100g are populated; no -1 sentinel values remain, so no sentinel documentation is required.
